text: Just as sets provide a convenient language for properties of the objects in a type, relations provide a convenient language for the properties of the pairs of objects in a type. This might sound dry and abstract, but such properties turn up all over mathematics: the property of one real number being less than another; the property of one integer being congruent to another modulo 5; the property of one set being a subset of another; the property of one function being inverse to another.
html: Just as sets provide a convenient language for properties of the objects in a type, relations provide a convenient language for the properties of the pairs of objects in a type. This might sound dry and abstract, but such properties turn up all over mathematics: the property of one real number being less than another; the property of one integer being congruent to another modulo 5; the property of one set being a subset of another; the property of one function being inverse to another.

text: In this chapter we introduce some of the important properties which relations themselves can have: they can be reflexive , symmetric , antisymmetric or transitive , or any combination of these.
html: In this chapter we introduce some of the important properties which relations themselves can have: they can be reflexive, symmetric, antisymmetric or transitive, or any combination of these.
Definition

text: A relation \(\sim\) on a type \(X\) is reflexive , if for all \(x\) of type \(X\) , it is true that \(x\sim x\) .
html: A relation \(\sim\) on a type \(X\) is reflexive, if for all \(x\) of type \(X\), it is true that \(x\sim x\).
Problem

text: Prove that the relation \(|\) on \(\mathbb{N}\) is reflexive.
html: Prove that the relation \(|\) on \(\mathbb{N}\) is reflexive.
Solution

text: We must show that for all natural numbers \(x\) , \(x\mid x\) . Indeed, let \(x\) be a natural number; then \(x=x\cdot 1\) .
html: We must show that for all natural numbers \(x\), \(x\mid x\). Indeed, let \(x\) be a natural number; then \(x=x\cdot 1\).
Definition

text: A relation \(\sim\) on a type \(X\) is symmetric , if for all \(x\) and \(y\) of type \(X\) , if \(x\sim y\) , then \(y\sim x\) .
html: A relation \(\sim\) on a type \(X\) is symmetric, if for all \(x\) and \(y\) of type \(X\), if \(x\sim y\), then \(y\sim x\).
Problem

text: Prove that the relation \(|\) on \(\mathbb{N}\) is not symmetric.
html: Prove that the relation \(|\) on \(\mathbb{N}\) is not symmetric.
Solution

text: We must show that there exist natural numbers \(x\) and \(y\) such that, \(x\mid y\) and \(y\mid x\) . Indeed, we have \(2=1\cdot 2\) , so \(1\mid 2\) , and \(2\cdot 0<1<2\cdot 1\) , so \(2\not \mid 1\) .
html: We must show that there exist natural numbers \(x\) and \(y\) such that, \(x\mid y\) and \(y\mid x\). Indeed, we have \(2=1\cdot 2\), so \(1\mid 2\), and \(2\cdot 0<1<2\cdot 1\), so \(2\not \mid 1\).
Definition

text: A relation \(\sim\) on a type \(X\) is antisymmetric , if for all \(x\) and \(y\) of type \(X\) , if \(x\sim y\) and \(y\sim x\) , then \(x=y\) .
html: A relation \(\sim\) on a type \(X\) is antisymmetric, if for all \(x\) and \(y\) of type \(X\), if \(x\sim y\) and \(y\sim x\), then \(x=y\).
Problem

text: Prove that the relation \(|\) on \(\mathbb{N}\) is antisymmetric.
html: Prove that the relation \(|\) on \(\mathbb{N}\) is antisymmetric.
Solution

text: We first note the following fact ( \(\star\) ): if \(m\) and \(n\) are natural numbers with \(m=0\) and \(m\mid n\) , then \(m=n\) . Indeed, since \(m\mid n\) there exists a natural number \(k\) such that \(n=mk\) , and then
html: We first note the following fact (\(\star\)): if \(m\) and \(n\) are natural numbers with \(m=0\) and \(m\mid n\), then \(m=n\). Indeed, since \(m\mid n\) there exists a natural number \(k\) such that \(n=mk\), and then

text: We now return to the original problem. We must show that for all natural numbers \(x\) and \(y\) , if \(x\mid y\) and \(y\mid x\) , then \(x=y\) . Indeed, let \(x\) and \(y\) be natural numbers and suppose \(x\mid y\) and \(y\mid x\) . If \(x=0\) then by ( \(\star\) ) we are done; likewise if \(y=0\) . Otherwise, \(x>0\) , so since \(y\mid x\) we have \(y\le x\) , and \(y>0\) , so since \(x\mid y\) we have \(x\le y\) , Putting these together, \(x=y\) .
html: We now return to the original problem. We must show that for all natural numbers \(x\) and \(y\), if \(x\mid y\) and \(y\mid x\), then \(x=y\). Indeed, let \(x\) and \(y\) be natural numbers and suppose \(x\mid y\) and \(y\mid x\). If \(x=0\) then by (\(\star\)) we are done; likewise if \(y=0\). Otherwise, \(x>0\), so since \(y\mid x\) we have \(y\le x\), and \(y>0\), so since \(x\mid y\) we have \(x\le y\), Putting these together, \(x=y\).
Definition

text: A relation \(\sim\) on a type \(X\) is transitive , if for all \(x\) , \(y\) and \(z\) of type \(X\) , if \(x\sim y\) and \(y\sim z\) , then \(x\sim z\) .
html: A relation \(\sim\) on a type \(X\) is transitive, if for all \(x\), \(y\) and \(z\) of type \(X\), if \(x\sim y\) and \(y\sim z\), then \(x\sim z\).
Problem

text: Prove that the relation \(|\) on \(\mathbb{N}\) is transitive.
html: Prove that the relation \(|\) on \(\mathbb{N}\) is transitive.
Solution

text: We must show that for all natural numbers \(a\) , \(b\) and \(c\) , if \(a\mid b\) and \(b\mid c\) then \(a\mid c\) .
html: We must show that for all natural numbers \(a\), \(b\) and \(c\), if \(a\mid b\) and \(b\mid c\) then \(a\mid c\).

text: Indeed, let \(a\) , \(b\) and \(c\) be natural numbers with these properties. Since \(a\mid b\) there exists a natural number \(k\) such that \(b=ak\) , and since \(b\mid c\) there exists a natural number \(l\) such that \(c=bl\) . We then have
html: Indeed, let \(a\), \(b\) and \(c\) be natural numbers with these properties. Since \(a\mid b\) there exists a natural number \(k\) such that \(b=ak\), and since \(b\mid c\) there exists a natural number \(l\) such that \(c=bl\). We then have
so \(a\mid c\).

text: Determine which of these properties hold for the relation \(=\) on \(\mathbb{R}\) :
html: Determine which of these properties hold for the relation \(=\) on \(\mathbb{R}\):

html: Determine which of these properties hold for the relation \(\sim\) on \(\mathbb{R}\), defined by, \(x\sim y\) if \((x-y)^2\leq 1\):

text: Consider the following finite inductive type Hand.
html: Consider the following finite inductive type Hand.

text: Consider the following relation \(\prec\) on the Hand type.
html: Consider the following relation \(\prec\) on the Hand type.

text: Determine which of these properties hold for the relation \(\prec\) :
html: Determine which of these properties hold for the relation \(\prec\):
Definition

text: A relation is an equivalence relation , if it is reflexive, symmetric and transitive.
html: A relation is an equivalence relation, if it is reflexive, symmetric and transitive.
Problem

text: Let \(n\) be an integer. Show that the relation \(\sim\) on \(\mathbb{Z}\) defined by, \(x\sim y\) if \(x\equiv y\mod n\) is an equivalence relation.
html: Let \(n\) be an integer. Show that the relation \(\sim\) on \(\mathbb{Z}\) defined by, \(x\sim y\) if \(x\equiv y\mod n\) is an equivalence relation.
Solution

text: By Example 3.3.10 , the relation \(\sim\) is reflexive.
html: By Example 3.3.10, the relation \(\sim\) is reflexive.

text: By two of the exercises to Section 3.3 , the relation \(\sim\) is symmetric and transitive.
html: By two of the exercises to Section 3.3, the relation \(\sim\) is symmetric and transitive.

text: Let’s sketch (a portion of) this relation as a directed graph, say for \(n=3\) .
html: Let’s sketch (a portion of) this relation as a directed graph, say for \(n=3\).

text: It’s a mess, but it’s just possible to make out a pattern: the numbers form cliques, with, for example, … -5, -2, 1, 4, 7, … all connected to each other and to nothing else. We introduce the following visual shorthand for such a directed graph: when nodes are given different colours, this represents the directed graph in which all nodes of a given colour are connected bi-directionally to all nodes of that colour (including themselves) and to no other nodes.
html: It’s a mess, but it’s just possible to make out a pattern: the numbers form cliques, with, for example, … -5, -2, 1, 4, 7, … all connected to each other and to nothing else. We introduce the following visual shorthand for such a directed graph: when nodes are given different colours, this represents the directed graph in which all nodes of a given colour are connected bi-directionally to all nodes of that colour (including themselves) and to no other nodes.
Problem

text: Show that the relation \(\sim\) on \(\mathbb{Z}\) defined by \(a\sim b\) if \(a^2=b^2\) is an equivalence relation.
html: Show that the relation \(\sim\) on \(\mathbb{Z}\) defined by \(a\sim b\) if \(a^2=b^2\) is an equivalence relation.
Solution

text: For all integers \(x\) , we have \(x^2=x^2\) , so \(x\sim x\) . Therefore the relation \(\sim\) is reflexive.
html: For all integers \(x\), we have \(x^2=x^2\), so \(x\sim x\). Therefore the relation \(\sim\) is reflexive.

text: For all integers \(x\) and \(y\) , we have that if \(x\sim y\) then \(x^2=y^2\) , so \(y^2=x^2\) , so \(y\sim z\) . Therefore the relation \(\sim\) is symmetric.
html: For all integers \(x\) and \(y\), we have that if \(x\sim y\) then \(x^2=y^2\), so \(y^2=x^2\), so \(y\sim z\). Therefore the relation \(\sim\) is symmetric.

text: For all integers \(x\) , \(y\) and \(z\) , we have that if \(x\sim y\) and \(y\sim z\) then \(x^2=y^2\) and \(y^2=z^2\) , so
html: For all integers \(x\), \(y\) and \(z\), we have that if \(x\sim y\) and \(y\sim z\) then \(x^2=y^2\) and \(y^2=z^2\), so

text: so \(x\sim z\) . Therefore the relation \(\sim\) is transitive.
html: so \(x\sim z\). Therefore the relation \(\sim\) is transitive.

text: If you try to sketch the directed graph for this relation, you will see that it has the same “clique” behaviour as the previous relation. After sketching the directed graph, sketch the multicoloured “clique” version.
html: If you try to sketch the directed graph for this relation, you will see that it has the same “clique” behaviour as the previous relation. After sketching the directed graph, sketch the multicoloured “clique” version.

text: You have probably guessed that the colouring can be made consistent in this way for any equivalence relation. Let’s make this mathematically rigorous.
html: You have probably guessed that the colouring can be made consistent in this way for any equivalence relation. Let’s make this mathematically rigorous.

text: Let \(r\) be a relation on a type \(\alpha\) , denoted \(\sim\) in infix notation.
html: Let \(r\) be a relation on a type \(\alpha\), denoted \(\sim\) in infix notation.
Definition

text: For \(a\) in \(\alpha\) , the equivalence class of \(a\) (denoted \([a]\) ) is \(\{b:\alpha\mid a\sim b\}\) .
html: For \(a\) in \(\alpha\), the equivalence class of \(a\) (denoted \([a]\)) is \(\{b:\alpha\mid a\sim b\}\).
Theorem

text: If the relation \(r\) is symmetric and transitive, then for all \(a_1\) and \(a_2\) , if \(a_1\sim a_2\) , then \([a_1]=[a_2]\) .
html: If the relation \(r\) is symmetric and transitive, then for all \(a_1\) and \(a_2\), if \(a_1\sim a_2\), then \([a_1]=[a_2]\).
Proof

text: We must show that for all \(b\) in \(\alpha\) , \(a_1\sim b\) if and only if \(a_2\sim b\) .
html: We must show that for all \(b\) in \(\alpha\), \(a_1\sim b\) if and only if \(a_2\sim b\).

text: First, suppose that \(a_1\sim b\) . Since \(a_1\sim a_2\) , by symmetry \(a_2\sim a_1\) , and so by transitivity \(a_2\sim a_1\sim b\) .
html: First, suppose that \(a_1\sim b\). Since \(a_1\sim a_2\), by symmetry \(a_2\sim a_1\), and so by transitivity \(a_2\sim a_1\sim b\).

text: Conversely, suppose that \(a_2\sim b\) . Then since \(a_1\sim a_2\) , by transitivity \(a_1\sim a_2\sim b\) .
html: Conversely, suppose that \(a_2\sim b\). Then since \(a_1\sim a_2\), by transitivity \(a_1\sim a_2\sim b\).
Theorem

text: If the relation \(r\) is reflexive, then every \(a\) is an element of its own equivalence class: \(a\in [a]\) .
html: If the relation \(r\) is reflexive, then every \(a\) is an element of its own equivalence class: \(a\in [a]\).
Proof

text: We must show that for all \(a\) , it is true that \(a\sim a\) , and this is the definition of reflexivity.
html: We must show that for all \(a\), it is true that \(a\sim a\), and this is the definition of reflexivity.

text: Consider the relation \(=\) on \(\mathbb{Z}\) . It is an equivalence relation, by Example 10.1.2 .
html: Consider the relation \(=\) on \(\mathbb{Z}\). It is an equivalence relation, by Example 10.1.2.

text: Exercise: Sketch this relation, by drawing (a portion of) the underlying type \(\mathbb{Z}\) along a line, then colouring each equivalence class in a different colour.
html: Exercise: Sketch this relation, by drawing (a portion of) the underlying type \(\mathbb{Z}\) along a line, then colouring each equivalence class in a different colour.
Problem

text: Show that the relation \(\sim\) on \(\mathbb{Z}\times\mathbb{N}\) defined by, \((a,b)\sim(c,d)\) if \(a(d+1)=c(b+1)\) , is an equivalence relation.
html: Show that the relation \(\sim\) on \(\mathbb{Z}\times\mathbb{N}\) defined by, \((a,b)\sim(c,d)\) if \(a(d+1)=c(b+1)\), is an equivalence relation.
Solution

text: For all \((a,b)\) in \(\mathbb{Z}\times\mathbb{N}\) , \(a(b+1)=a(b+1)\) , so \((a,b)\sim (a,b)\) . Therefore \(\sim\) is reflexive.
html: For all \((a,b)\) in \(\mathbb{Z}\times\mathbb{N}\), \(a(b+1)=a(b+1)\), so \((a,b)\sim (a,b)\). Therefore \(\sim\) is reflexive.

text: For all \((a,b)\) and \((c,d)\) in \(\mathbb{Z}\times\mathbb{N}\) , if \((a,b)\sim (c,d)\) then \(a(d+1)=c(b+1)\) , so \(c(b+1)=a(d+1)\) , so \((c,d)\sim (a,b)\) . Therefore \(\sim\) is symmetric.
html: For all \((a,b)\) and \((c,d)\) in \(\mathbb{Z}\times\mathbb{N}\), if \((a,b)\sim (c,d)\) then \(a(d+1)=c(b+1)\), so \(c(b+1)=a(d+1)\), so \((c,d)\sim (a,b)\). Therefore \(\sim\) is symmetric.

text: For all \((a,b)\) , \((c,d)\) and \((e,f)\) in \(\mathbb{Z}\times\mathbb{N}\) , if \((a,b)\sim (c,d)\) and \((c,d)\sim (e,f)\) then \(a(d+1)=c(b+1)\) and \(c(f+1)=e(d+1)\) . We will show that \(a(f+1)=e(b+1)\) , which will imply that \((a,b)\sim (e,f)\) , which proves the transitivity of \(\sim\) .
html: For all \((a,b)\), \((c,d)\) and \((e,f)\) in \(\mathbb{Z}\times\mathbb{N}\), if \((a,b)\sim (c,d)\) and \((c,d)\sim (e,f)\) then \(a(d+1)=c(b+1)\) and \(c(f+1)=e(d+1)\). We will show that \(a(f+1)=e(b+1)\), which will imply that \((a,b)\sim (e,f)\), which proves the transitivity of \(\sim\).

text: Since \(d+1>0\) , it suffices to prove that \((d+1)\left[a(f+1)\right]=(d+1)\left[e(b+1)\right]\) . Let \(B:= b+1\) , \(D:= d+1\) , and \(F:= f+1\) : then we know that \(aD=cB\) and \(cF=eD\) and need to prove that \(D(aF)=D(eB)\) .
html: Since \(d+1>0\), it suffices to prove that \((d+1)\left[a(f+1)\right]=(d+1)\left[e(b+1)\right]\). Let \(B:= b+1\), \(D:= d+1\), and \(F:= f+1\): then we know that \(aD=cB\) and \(cF=eD\) and need to prove that \(D(aF)=D(eB)\).
Indeed,

text: Now, sketch the relation \(\sim\) , by drawing (a portion of) the underlying type \(\mathbb{Z}\times\mathbb{N}\) in the plane, then colouring each equivalence class in a different colour.
html: Now, sketch the relation \(\sim\), by drawing (a portion of) the underlying type \(\mathbb{Z}\times\mathbb{N}\) in the plane, then colouring each equivalence class in a different colour.
Theorem

text: The relation \(\sim\) on the type of level-0 types defined by, \(\alpha\sim\beta\) if there exists a bijective function \(f:\alpha\to\beta\) , is an equivalence relation.
html: The relation \(\sim\) on the type of level-0 types defined by, \(\alpha\sim\beta\) if there exists a bijective function \(f:\alpha\to\beta\), is an equivalence relation.

text: Consider the following finite inductive type Little, and the following relation \(\sim\) on the Little type. Determine which of these properties hold for the relation \(\sim\) : reflexive; symmetric; antisymmetric; transitive. section inductive Little | meg | jo | beth | amy deriving DecidableEq open Little @[reducible] def s : Little → Little → Prop | meg , meg => True | meg , jo => True | meg , beth => True | meg , amy => True | jo , meg => True | jo , jo => True | jo , beth => True | jo , amy => False | beth , meg => True | beth , jo => True | beth , beth => False | beth , amy => True | amy , meg => True | amy , jo => False | amy , beth => True | amy , amy => True local infix : 50 " ∼ " => s example : Reflexive ( · ∼ · ) := by sorry example : ¬ Reflexive ( · ∼ · ) := by sorry example : Symmetric ( · ∼ · ) := by sorry example : ¬ Symmetric ( · ∼ · ) := by sorry example : AntiSymmetric ( · ∼ · ) := by sorry example : ¬ AntiSymmetric ( · ∼ · ) := by sorry example : Transitive ( · ∼ · ) := by sorry example : ¬ Transitive ( · ∼ · ) := by sorry
html:
• Consider the following finite inductive type Little, and the following relation \(\sim\) on the Little type. Determine which of these properties hold for the relation \(\sim\):

    1. reflexive;

    2. symmetric;

    3. antisymmetric;

    4. transitive.

    section
inductive Little
  | meg
  | jo
  | beth
  | amy
  deriving DecidableEq

open Little

@[reducible] def s : Little → Little → Prop
  | meg, meg => True
  | meg, jo => True
  | meg, beth => True
  | meg, amy => True
  | jo, meg => True
  | jo, jo => True
  | jo, beth => True
  | jo, amy => False
  | beth, meg => True
  | beth, jo => True
  | beth, beth => False
  | beth, amy => True
  | amy, meg => True
  | amy, jo => False
  | amy, beth => True
  | amy, amy => True

local infix:50 " ∼ " => s

    example : Reflexive (· ∼ ·) := by
  sorry

example : ¬ Reflexive (· ∼ ·) := by
  sorry

example : Symmetric (· ∼ ·) := by
  sorry

example : ¬ Symmetric (· ∼ ·) := by
  sorry

example : AntiSymmetric (· ∼ ·) := by
  sorry

example : ¬ AntiSymmetric (· ∼ ·) := by
  sorry

example : Transitive (· ∼ ·) := by
  sorry

example : ¬ Transitive (· ∼ ·) := by
  sorry

text: Consider the relation \(\sim\) on \(\mathbb{Z}\) defined by, \(a\sim b\) if there exist positive integers \(m\) and \(n\) such that \(am=bn\) . Show that \(\sim\) is an equivalence relation. Sketch the relation \(\sim\) , by drawing (a portion of) the underlying type \(\mathbb{Z}\) along a line, then colouring each equivalence class in a different colour. local infix : 50 "∼" => fun ( a b : ℤ ) ↦ ∃ m n , m > 0 ∧ n > 0 ∧ a * m = b * n example : Reflexive ( · ∼ · ) := by sorry example : Symmetric ( · ∼ · ) := by sorry example : Transitive ( · ∼ · ) := by sorry

text: Consider the relation \(\sim\) on \(\mathbb{Z}^2\) defined by, \((a,b)\sim(c,d)\) if there exist positive integers \(m\) and \(n\) with \(mb(b^2-3a^2)=nd(d^2-3c^2)\) . Show that \(\sim\) is an equivalence relation. Sketch the relation \(\sim\) , by drawing (a portion of) the underlying type \(\mathbb{Z}^2\) in the plane, then colouring each equivalence class in a different colour. local infix : 50 "∼" => fun (( a , b ) : ℤ × ℤ ) ( c , d ) ↦ ∃ m n , m > 0 ∧ n > 0 ∧ m * b * ( b ^ 2 - 3 * a ^ 2 ) = n * d * ( d ^ 2 - 3 * c ^ 2 ) example : Reflexive ( · ∼ · ) := by sorry example : Symmetric ( · ∼ · ) := by sorry example : Transitive ( · ∼ · ) := by sorry
html:
• Consider the relation \(\sim\) on \(\mathbb{Z}^2\) defined by, \((a,b)\sim(c,d)\) if there exist positive integers \(m\) and \(n\) with \(mb(b^2-3a^2)=nd(d^2-3c^2)\).

    • Show that \(\sim\) is an equivalence relation.

    • Sketch the relation \(\sim\), by drawing (a portion of) the underlying type \(\mathbb{Z}^2\) in the plane, then colouring each equivalence class in a different colour.

    local infix:50 "∼" => fun ((a, b) : ℤ × ℤ) (c, d) ↦
  ∃ m n, m > 0 ∧ n > 0 ∧ m * b * (b ^ 2 - 3 * a ^ 2) = n * d * (d ^ 2 - 3 * c ^ 2)
